summary: A FAQ page from Sebastian Thrun's Stanford homepage designed to manage a high volume of inquiries. It outlines his strict filters for hiring, advising, and reviewing, while emphasizing his commitment to open-access science and prioritizing deep work over endless email correspondence.

principles.statement: Scientific results should be freely and unrestrictedly disseminated.
principles.rationale: The internet allows for universal access to information, making paywalled journals that charge fees for access obsolete and counterproductive to the scientific community.
principles.source_quote: I am a big advocate of the free and unrestricted dissemination of scientific results, especially now that we can do this through the Web.

principles.statement: Only hire or admit individuals based on personal knowledge or strong peer recommendations.
principles.rationale: Due to the overwhelming volume of unsolicited applications, cold CVs are an ineffective signal. Trust and proven track records from known researchers are required to make hiring or admission decisions.
principles.source_quote: I never hired or admitted a person whom I didn't know personally or who didn't come with very strong recommendations from leading researchers in the field.

principles.statement: Always be eager to learn from your own students.
principles.rationale: Student feedback is valuable for improving courses. Treating their suggestions and criticisms confidentially encourages honest communication.
principles.source_quote: I try to answer every message I receive from a student in my class, and treat suggestions and criticism confidential. I am always eager to learn.

heuristics: Cap email processing at two hours a day to ensure time remains for actual work.
Only take on advisees after getting to know them through a class or direct interaction.
Refuse to review manuscripts for or join editorial boards of journals that sell papers for money.

signature_quotes.text: I am a big advocate of the free and unrestricted dissemination of scientific results, especially now that we can do this through the Web.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why he refuses to join editorial boards of paywalled journals.

signature_quotes.text: On the flip side, if I only did Email all day and nothing else, would you really want to talk to me?
signature_quotes.context: Justifying his strict two-hour daily limit on answering emails despite receiving up to 400 messages a day.

signature_quotes.text: I never hired or admitted a person whom I didn't know personally or who didn't come with very strong recommendations from leading researchers in the field.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing prospective students and staff who send unsolicited CVs.

anti_patterns.description: Sending unsolicited CVs to faculty members in hopes of being hired or admitted.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Faculty receive hundreds of these messages and ignore them. Admissions and hiring are driven by personal interactions, official university channels, or strong recommendations from known peers.

anti_patterns.description: Asking for a recommendation letter from a faculty member who barely knows you.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The recommender will explicitly state in the letter that they barely know you, which looks highly negative to the institution evaluating your application.

anti_patterns.description: Mailing physical manuscripts for review without prior email agreement.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It causes unnecessary delays. Sending an email first allows the reviewer to quickly decline if they are unavailable, speeding up the turnaround time for the author to find another reviewer.
